{"status":"ok","message-type":"work","message-version":"1.0.0","message":{"indexed":{"date-parts":[[2026,6,19]],"date-time":"2026-06-19T20:25:02Z","timestamp":1781900702827,"version":"3.54.5"},"reference-count":37,"publisher":"Oxford University Press (OUP)","issue":"6","license":[{"start":{"date-parts":[[2025,12,9]],"date-time":"2025-12-09T00:00:00Z","timestamp":1765238400000},"content-version":"vor","delay-in-days":36,"URL":"https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/"}],"funder":[{"name":"Ministry of Health (Singapore) Health Innovation","award":["MH 110:12/12-30"],"award-info":[{"award-number":["MH 110:12/12-30"]}]}],"content-domain":{"domain":[],"crossmark-restriction":false},"short-container-title":[],"published-print":{"date-parts":[[2025,11,3]]},"abstract":"<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Neonatal jaundice monitoring is resource-intensive. Existing artificial intelligence methods use image or clinical data, but none systematically combine both or compare feature contributions. This study fills that gap by extracting and analyzing multimodal features on a large dataset, identifying an optimal feature set for accurate, accessible jaundice assessment.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Materials and Methods</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>This study collected clinical data and skin images from 3 body regions of 633 neonates, generating 460 features across 4 categories. Four tree-based models were used to predict total serum bilirubin levels and feature importance analysis guided the selection of an optimal feature set.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>The optimal performance was achieved using the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM) model with 140 selected features, yielding a root mean square error (RMSE) of 2.0477 mg/dL and a Pearson correlation of 0.8435. This represents a performance gain of over 10% in RMSE compared to models using only a single data modality. Moreover, selecting the top 30 features based on SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) allows for a substantial reduction in data dimensionality, while maintaining performance within 5% of the optimal model.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Discussion</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Color features contributed over 60% of the total importance, with clinical data adding more than 25%, led by hour of life. Light temperature also affected predictions, while texture features had minimal impact. Among body regions, the abdomen provided the most informative signals for jaundice severity.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>The proposed algorithm shows promise for real-world use by enabling timely, automated jaundice assessment for families, while also offering insights for future research and broader medical applications.</jats:p>
